item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks very much :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it a lot. The distinction made between in universe uncertainty as to who is controlling this thing combined with the nod and wink to we, the readers, is a really nice touch. Good job well done! +1

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
I love the idea. Yes you're right that the CI tie is intended to be subtle - the Foundation is not supposed to be aware who is in control of the object.

A thought - SCP-140 would probably be the only object that 1275 would have been found attempting to steal at Site-76, given that had others gone missing security would have already been increased. But there's actually LOTS of objects at Site-76 if you do a search - SCP-1634, SCP-1971, SCP-1068, SCP-2061, etc etc.

Currently only SCP-140 and SCP-1883 are mentioned on the Secure Facilities page. Perhaps you could list all the objects contained at Site-76 on the Secure Facilities page, and then make "Site-76" on this page into a link to that page? There are other sites listed there with more complete listings of the objects contained - for instance one of them has 12 separate entries listed. So this wouldn't be something unusual.

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Normally I'd suggest an inclusion of a link to the CI hub in a CI article, but since I believe the author is shooting for subtlety with regards to the CI, we'll forgo that for now. Since this article involves a smash and grab at Site-76, why not rework some of the black box skips to some notable Site-76 articles, like SCP-140. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

08/15/2003: Still-frame analysis systems at Site-76 monitoring camera feeds outside SCP-███'s containment chamber found irregularities between frames and proceeded to initiate a lockdown of the area. Upon close examination, a small fluid object appeared to be attempting to enter SCP-███'s containment cell. A security team was dispatched to investigate, resulting in the loss of all three members. Containment attempts resulted in the loss of a further seven security personnel and two agents before it could be brought under control.

To this:

08/15/2003: Still-frame analysis systems at Site-76 monitoring camera feeds outside SCP-140's containment chamber found irregularities between frames and proceeded to initiate a lockdown of the area. Upon close examination, a small fluid object appeared to be attempting to enter SCP-140's containment cell. A security team was dispatched to investigate, resulting in the loss of all three members. Containment attempts resulted in the loss of a further seven security personnel and two agents before it could be brought under control.

If there is any other articles suitable for inclusion here for notable theft by the CI, I welcome others to mention them below.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For image team purposes:

Image borrowed and modified from https://www.flickr.com/photos/94941635@N07/8708021482/

Released under Creative Commons license CC BY 2.0. Per the terms of said license, the license is not modified and cannot be modified, regardless of the SCP wiki's CC BY-SA 3.0 license.

Artist: Zoltán Vörös, taken April 28, 2013.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought I had replied to all important questions in the comments but somehow I skipped right over this one.

Why in the Gobi desert? Boulder Smolder has it right. The Gobi desert has large areas of rocky terrain and compact sand. That's the type of area it is contained in. It can't just slip into the sand and sneak away. If it goes down into a crack it's more likely to end up in a limited space with no idea where it can go from there. It's not omniscient here, it can't predict what obstacles it's going to run into.

750cm tungsten cube: it's a cube, so yes each side is 750cm. As for thickness I felt that wouldn't be relevant to the reader's understanding of the article - but I did decide on a thickness when I wrote this. 50cm thick. The idea here is to be strong enough to resist the vast majority of potential attacks that might be launched from within.

*edit* and this is where I come to the horrified realization that I meant mm, not cm….I mean I suppose something about 25 feet long on each side and about 2 feet thick would do a pretty good job of resisting containment breach efforts…but calculating that out, that's about $120 million of tungsten and $30 million of lead….yeah, no. Editing to fix the longstanding error. x_x

Electrified tungsten wire grid: the purpose of the grid is to divide the interior space into cubic millimeter sections, to prevent the entity from bringing anything bigger than 1 cubic millimeter into the cube at any given time. It is electrified for the purpose of shorting out/destroying anything that does come through as quickly as possible(such as transmitters/GPS devices/etc). It is presumed that nothing the size of a cubic millimeter is powerful enough to breach its containment through other means.

Why does the lead need to be molten? So that 1275 can't create a hole for itself over time by slowly removing pieces of the material surrounding it. Molten lead will fill in any gaps it creates. It is continually filled to prevent it from draining its containment as well. Lead blocks any possible transmissions or radiation as well.

Video feed is of its chamber to ensure that there's no damage to the cube.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Image borrowed and modified from https://www.flickr.com/photos/94941635@N07/8708021482/

Released under Creative Commons license CC BY 2.0. Per the terms of said license, the license is not modified and cannot be modified, regardless of the SCP wiki's CC BY-SA 3.0 license.

Artist: Zoltán Vörös, taken April 28, 2013.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's an authorial conceit of sorts. The object IS operated by the Chaos Insurgency, and no the Foundation doesn't know that, but I want the audience to be able to know that. Most should be able to figure that out from the information provided, but I wanted it unambiguous so people didn't make up other organizations for it. The tags are not purported to be placed there by the in-universe Foundation, after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought it is mentioned that too little info is known to the Foundation and hence can't link 1275 to any known affiliation? Why is there a tag to chaos insurgency?

I mean, 1275 sounds exactly like something that Chaos Insurgency will create so that they can grasp SCPs and use it themselves… But it just kinda bugs me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can't really disagree with you there.

The intent was to give something of a timeline of major events to give the rationale behind the existing containment procedures along with giving the reader the clues needed to make the Chaos Insurgency connection. But I do tend to get a bit wordy and if I can think of a decent way to trim it down sometime without losing the narrative details, I might.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really like the containment procedures and feel the object is powerful without going truly over the top. However, I do feel the middle of this article is actively bogged down by the logs which bog down to "site was attacked, stuff happened, then attack stopped".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The electricity and heat are not for the direct containment of the entity, but rather to short out/destroy anything that the entity might manage to bring through. With the wire mesh in the box it cannot bring through anything bigger than one cubic millimeter to begin with, and the hope is that the heat, pressure, and electricity will be enough to destroy anything that it does manage to bring through before it can do anything.

Along with this, the lead and tungsten were both chosen for their chemical non-reactivity, the lead was chosen for its low melting temperature and radiation shielding properties, the tungsten was chosen for its high melting temperature, durability/hardness, and conductivity, and the room has a blast shielding rating just in case it does manage to bring explosives through that are powerful enough to destroy the box.

They cannot affect the entity itself directly, no, and they know this. But the entity cannot breach its containment of its own accord either. If that were the only concern, they could just lock it in an airtight box and that'd be the end of it. The entire problem centers around its capacity for disgorging objects that might breach its containment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The containment procedures don't make sense to me given that the entity is said to be electrically nonconductive and not reactive to temperature changes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1. Upvote just for the containment procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cleaned up the phrasing in a couple places. I really like this!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just wanted to comment about the pressure rating. 200 kpa is a little low if you're designing this thing to resist the pressures generated by high energy explosions. For comparison, steam piping can have a design pressure of 1900 kpa, and that's not even Keter. You may want to revise those figures upward.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see, I see, hmmm…

Wouldn't the molten lead be enough to destroy the electronics just as quickly as shorting them out? I don't have a lot of experience with hardened electronics, but I can't imagine 1 mm3 allows much room for heat shielding.

I second Jack's suggestion above, that would be good information to have straight off the bat. There should also be a line about checking the volume of lead and replacing as needed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This bothers me:

"SCP-1275 is contained at Containment Area 43"

It just screams of redundancy. It kind of hurts to read. Other than that, I have to read the rest of it first. I got to that point and had to come here to post that. Regardless, it's highly voted so I'll give it a chance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm still having issues with the term "extradimensionally" being put in. It also still doesn't mention that the mesh grid (or something about the mesh grid) limits its behavior in the description, which I think would be a significant detail.

Either way, you have my upvote now. I ain't got beef with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After thinking it over on my own for a while, I'm pretty much in agreement with you- a writer more clever than I would best put such in a Tale, probably approved by a 7-6 vote for the reasons you listed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In this instance, the only time period where the Foundation could have attempted this was during the four month period when it was inert. It didn't try to envelop anything during that time, and that's when they did most of their tests and studies on it, though they ultimately didn't learn much of anything during that time. During its escape attempts, it wasn't focused on trying to capture Foundation materials, only on escape. The only time that it could have been attempted would have been by placing tracers on personnel it sucked in…..suffice to say that at the time, they had more pressing matters on their mind.

Fact is, they don't want to put it in any situation where it might be able to release anything of substantial size. They fear that the instant it's got space to bring stuff through, it'll find a way to bring something through that breaches its containment and/or completely destroys the site. It's Keter for two reasons: because of how difficult it is to keep it reliably contained, and because of the hazard it presents to the Foundation if it was free to steal Foundation materials and personnel again. These things merit forbidding it any kind of freedom to envelop even a Foundation trap.

That said, I'm ok with the idea of this being explored in a Tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I already like the concept and execution enough to give it my upvote. There's just one thing I'd like to see expanded on, though- if it refuses to accept equipment (i.e. almost certainly surveillance technology) lest it wants to, why not trick it into wanting to? Something along the lines of letting 1275 consume false intelligence regarding a site holding an SCP that is simply a bleeding-edge means of spying on them. Getting valuable intel on a powerful GOI that has a nasty habit of stealing from us, killing most of them, then finding a way to destroy their link to us would go a long way toward our raison d'être. I know the Foundation would rather preserve the anomalous than destroy it but we do tend to make exceptions against Keter objects that want to fuck our shit completely up, 682 being the obvious example and this being a more direct instance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Gobi Desert isn't actually sandy. Most of the terrain is bare rock.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because of what emerged from inside it. Either it was capable of spontaneously generating people and explosives, or they were coming from somewhere else. Given that a transmitter came out and shortly after that a recovery team was intercepted enroute to recover it, it seemed pretty likely that that organization was the one that sent the transmitter. If it was capable of spontaneous generation of not just objects but seemingly living beings and even objects dangerous enough to be scp items, that'd be even worse in its way. But then why would it have stopped?

In short - observation and deduction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You say it's potentially sentient or controlled by a sentient force, then say it's controlled by a hostile group. We can figure out that second part, so it's not necessary to say and would cut down on one theory.

Also, how in the heck can something move 'extradimensionally'? How did they come to that conclusion?

No vote for now, but this is pretty good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you, I just might do that. I was trying to figure what the proper term would be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So are you imagining a 3-d grid that fills the box? If so, maybe describe it as, "a 3-dimensional tungsten wire lattice which divides the cube into 1 mm3 sections". That would clarify the shape as well as give an early clue to its purpose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My issue with this is all this 'theorizing' done by the article itself. I can decide what is actually happening in the object, especially after reading the addendum. I don't need sentences at the end of each paragraph giving me what J-J-Jeff Goldblum thinks about the object's behavior.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
See how it slurps
Like pancakes and syr'p
See how it breaks containment
I'm mehing

(GOO GOO GOO JOO)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The grid is interior, yes.

The mesh interrupts the volume, yes. It doesn't have room to produce anything of a size substantial enough to break itself free. It is electrified to short out and destroy electronics that may be sent through. The lead is molten to destroy other objects that may be sent through, to prevent it from hollowing out a hole for itself, and to ensure that any material it does remove from the box is quickly and easily replenished. Lead and tungsten were chosen for being non-reactive, lead's electromagnetic shielding properties, and tungsten's toughness.

The blob fits in because the blob was there before the lead was added. The lead doesn't fill in the blob, it just surrounds it and keeps it from being able to move.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I like it, but containment could use some polish.

-I'm thinking the electified grid is interior to the cube, not embedded in the material of the sides, but it could be read either way.

-Does the mesh work like a farady cage, or is it the fact that the blob doesn't have enough uninterrupted volume to put arriving stuff in? If the former, put it outside the cube and remove a potential point of failure. If the later, why does it need to be electrified?

-if the lead is mostly for broadcast shielding, why does it need to be molten?

-if all the remaining space is filled with molten lead, where does the blob fit in? Can it use its extradimensionality to overlap with mass already occupying a space? If so, then what's to keep it from shadowcat-ing out of any box we use? If not, then there needs to be some minimum volume left for it in the cube.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It has been revised and rearranged largely based upon your feedback. Let me know if things still seem confused/forced/to have bad tone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, I didn't know that. Thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Most/all keyboards in the US have a key for ~, but not one for ≈, so it's easier to type. Also, in science/tech/math, ≈ is used in equations (2+2≈5, for sufficiently large values of 2), whereas ~ is used when doing stand-alone approximations (there are ~2 people in this room)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1275 is contained at Containment Area 43, located in the Gobi Desert.

Why in the Gobi Desert, considering that it has sand which is can burrow through and hide under? Wouldn't they want it contained at a place where the terrain is just bare rock?

It is to be contained within a 750 cm tungsten cube,

1) Each side of the cube is 750 cm long?

2) How thick is this cube?

containing an electrified tungsten wire grid

The wire grid should be outside/surrounding the cube, since that makes it easier to do maintenance on it, plus that way the cube doesn't need any holes in it for the wires to supply the current.

the remaining space being filled with lead maintained at 1200°C.

Why does the lead need to be molten?

SCP-1275 is to be constantly monitored via sonar, video feed,

If it's inside a tungsten cube which is itself filled with molten lead, I don't think they'd be able to get a video feed of it.

The mesh grid has prevented SCP-1275 from disgorging objects capable of breaching its containment,

1) So being surrounded by an electrical current prevents it from disgorging objects?

2) I don't think "capable of breaching its containment" is needed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is there a reason people prefer ~ to ≈?
Is it a site standard or what?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah ha! I get it now, I had my mental image wrong. Take a belated +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not able to envelop something it's contained within. And if the seams are tight enough, it can't get out. It can't just spontaneously generate bombs, the group on the other side has to decide to send stuff through. It's definitely difficult to contain, and very hazardous, but it's not that fast and it's not that strong. I'm glad that you understand the dangers it presents, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This seems a bit too powerful. The Foundation might be able to contain it once they've got it, but I have no idea how they transport it, recapture it, or anything else. This works if it's in containment, but I legitimately can't see how something that slippery with no apparent weaknesses could be captured at all. It's fairly well-written, and I like the hint of CI, but I'm not quite sure if it works overall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ohhhhhhh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: S'all good. Looking forward to its improvement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I always assumed that SCP-xxxx just meant Special Containment Procedures for xxxx, so that the prefix refers to the file and not the entity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do like the object. There are some iffy tonal things going on ("Hostilities commenced, containment was breached" could be replaced with something like "The hostile forces initiated an attack against Foundation personnel; containment on SCP-1275 was breached shortly thereafter"; while obviously more verbose, it's also more clinical, whereas the current phrasing is…bored-sounding, I guess? As if the paragraph is just rattling through plot points before getting to the good stuff.), but it's a good object. No vote for just little things I can't fully put my finger on. It almost seems, weird though it sounds, like this is the X-man of transdimensional metallic goop. It's just a little too powerful to not be able to, say, send itself through itself.

Nice slipping in the CI tag, though. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks very much :) your feedback will take a while to incorporate properly, but I feel I can definitely improve it using this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Click for Full Review
Hide Full Review

Under no circumstances may personnel approach within 30m of SCP-1275 if it is loose, recontainment will be accomplished via specialized capture netting, delivered by air

This is a really awkward sentence. It really is. Could do with some fixing, preferably something about the comma between 'if it is loose' and 'recontainment'.

SCP-1275 seems not to exist fully within the material world.

This seems like quite a logical jump when you think about it, especially so much if it's considered important enough to mention in the beginning of the description.

Parts separated from the main body appear to flow extradimensionally

…extradimensionally? That sounds so cheesed. Hell, even 'through an unknown means' would be better than that.

objects of value

This is extremely vague. Value according to whom? If I thought that my dog was valuable, would it be enveloped too? Valuable to the Foundation? So D-class would be ignored?

will vanish to parts unknown

This also seems really really cheesed and iffy tone. You could better state that they have vanished without any successful recovery thus far, or something.

the object enveloped
Objects and personnel taken

I see that this is inconsistent. If you're going to call them 'the objects enveloped' stick to that.

However, evidence now suggests that SCP-1275 has an end point in another part of the world that it is connected to extradimensionally, and that it shifts portions of itself between these endpoints, accounting for its apparent fluctuations in volume.

Subtle as a brick to the face. I also have no idea how any evidence could suggest this. I mean, jesus hell man. If that evidence existed, it would be more fun to have it put in the article in a way that the reader could infer it than having it slammed into them like this. Also, I am becoming progressively more and more unimpressed with this article and have yet to see why it would be a keter.

The entity appears unable to remove the endpoint held by the foundation. It is theorized that either this is the primary half of the entity, or that it naturally has a divided existence.

…What? The Foundation has an endpoint? This wasn't mentioned in the beginning of the description at all? So confused. What do you mean remove? Like, pick it up and move it around? What is the endpoint?

deemed to be of value

…By who? By it? It has knowledge of the Foundation? This isn't mentioned as to how it could acquire this information? It's not even registered as through an unknown means?

presumably transporting them to its other endpoint

What evidence is there for that claim? I just don't get it at this point and it sounds very thrown-together.

Evidence

What evidence? It sounds so cheesed right now.

the entity sides with

Uhm… I don't like this tone.

is controlled by the hostile agents

What hostile agents? Hostile agents were mentioned in the article? Am I reading this wrong?

To date too little data has been recovered to establish a definite link to any individual group.

I don't understand, this link to a random hostile group comes out of nowhere.

though apparently none to itself

The fact it can 'disgorge' high energy explosives should be an important fact mentioned in the description, methinks. I mean, that is fairly important.

Entity was moved

Oh come on, not even 'the' now? I don't approve. I also have an issue with the whole 'the entity'. I would much prefer it to be more 'SCP-XXXX' in there, or something. If it's like multiple sentences in a row of SCP-XXXX, that's fine to replace one with 'the entity', but instead we have the exact opposite problem in which 'the entity' is refered to again and again.

An unknown hostile retrieval team

OH! THERE THE HOSTILE AGENTS ARE! Why wasn't this explained previously? Why would you confuse the reader by specifying things that aren't even gone into until later in the article, but refuse to put in big details like BEING ABLE TO DISGORGE EXPLOSIVES AND SCPS in the description?

enemy agents armed with paranormal weaponry emerged from within SCP-1275

Wait, so, are we talking about these hostile agents or the other hostile agents? Also, what idiot thinks that's a good idea? This isn't a video game, you don't just set up base in the enemy's base and expect it to work.

driven back into SCP-1275 again

It was driven back previously? Also, 'driven back' seems like very odd tone.

The mesh grid has prevented SCP-1275 from disgorging objects capable of breaching its containment

The fact that the SCP not only disgorges objects, but is prevented from doing so by a mesh grid seems like something that would be included in the description rather than vague allusions to ~mysterious enemy agents~ and ~a portal to another part of the world~ that isn't made clear or even explained until the last few bits. Honestly, even a (refer to Addendum-XXXX-BUTT) would've been better to the lack any explanation or warning whatsoever.

has thus far prevented transmitters from signaling its location to enemy agents

Wait, how do we know this? Does it have transmitters? Not sure about the whole 'enemy agents' thing now that I think about it too.

Overall, it seems like a very confused article. Important details are specified in the beginning without context and then the context is shoved into the addendum in the kind of forced manner that resembles "…and then he woke up". There are multiple important details about what the object does that aren't at all mentioned in the description, which is a problem for me. I also find issue with multiple pieces of tone and sentence structures. Overall, don't like the article. I think that the "reveal" is actually really cool, and the idea of some organization purposefully trying to sabotage the Foundation from within also cool. But its badly organized right now and me liking for what you were trying to do is outweighed by my dislike of how you did it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
parts unknown

Also, meh. It's written well for the most part, but I don't like the concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you. Most of the issues here are now addressed. As for those that are not, I will at least say why.

"Affected by gravity, yet registers no weight." That's what gravity is.

Weight is a measurement of an object's mass as affected by gravity. It is pulled downward by gravity, but does not register any weight upon scales/other instruments. It does not return any tactile sensation when touched. These give something of an idea of its properties.

"Hostilities began, containment was breached." Poor tone, bad comma.

Not sure how to rephrase this one.

The recovery log is one paragraph of recovery log followed by two unrelated to its recovery: the second paragraph is about its inferred or deduced behavior prior to containment, while the third is about the Foundation's exploratory interactions with it after containment.

The recovery log deals with more than simply the recovery, yes, it is the Foundation's initial information they were able to ascertain about it. I felt it applicable. I may rework this later though, I do feel you have a point.

The Incident Log is not about a single incident, but instead comprises over 19 months of behavior including at least four or five unique events worthy of being called "incidents."

I was initially going to format this as a table, but it felt wrong to me. Rather than being a group of isolated incidents, all of the incidents are connected as a prolonged attempt at escape/recovery.

The similarity to 1322 is one that I had noted and worried about, but knowing the actual story and background to it I know that it's a very different set of circumstances, so I was willing to move forward with it. Agree that 1322 is better executed though, that's one of the best on the site in my opinion.

That all said - I am impressed at your style of analysis and feedback. Keep it up with others, this was good work and very helpful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can't get behind this, for a lot of small reasons.

  • There are just enough issues with tone and grammar to be noticeable as an issue overall. For instance,
    • "Under no circumstances may personnel approach within 30m of SCP-1275 if it is loose, recontainment will be accomplished via specialized capture netting, delivered by air." Run-on sentence, first comma should be a period or semicolon.
    • Mixed usage of "re-containment" and "recontainment."
    • "Affected by gravity, yet registers no weight." That's what gravity is.
    • "the object will vanish to parts unknown." Bad tone.
    • "Hostilities began, containment was breached." Poor tone, bad comma.
    • The text to re-collapse the two collapsibles are inconsistent: -Hide vs - hide block.
    • etc.
  • The recovery log is one paragraph of recovery log followed by two unrelated to its recovery: the second paragraph is about its inferred or deduced behavior prior to containment, while the third is about the Foundation's exploratory interactions with it after containment.
  • The recovery log says "the entity sides with or is controlled by the hostile agents" (emphasis mine), but you haven't yet introduced any hostile agents except to suggest that SCP-1275 may be "controlled by an outside intelligence." Did you originally have the Incident Report and Recovery Log swapped?
  • "Internal breach efforts" by whom? SCP-1275 or the "enemy agents"?
  • The Incident Log is not about a single incident, but instead comprises over 19 months of behavior including at least four or five unique events worthy of being called "incidents."

In particular, the back half of this piece reads more like a story/tale than a containment document, and the escalation of hostilities feels like SCP-1322 but not as well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Key difference - this one isn't connecting to an alternate dimension. The things coming out of it are brought there from somewhere else in THIS world. 354 didn't even come to mind, but now that it's mentioned I can assure you they're really nothing alike.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The changes you've made are enough for me to move to an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Done. In most instances anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not exactly sure on this one.
Probably because I can't throw sulfur on it to see what happens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You can just use the word "it" if you feel like you're getting repetitive. Or adjust the wording so you don't have to refer to the SCP by name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm. I was actually specifically avoiding that because saying SCP-1275 over and over sounded highly repetitive. But perhaps I need to get a better balance and change a few over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just call it SCP-1275.

"SCP-1275 is a big blob whatsit. Sometimes it goes into the SCP Foundation and jacks their shit. This made the SCP Foundation sad, so they contained SCP-1275. Now it wants out."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No vote. Can't shake the feeling that this is somehow too similar to SCP-354. I like it overall but it's main concept is a dimensional link. I do like the energy explosives and the apparent sentience though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Out of curiosity, what would you suggest referring to it as?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea, but there's too many little things in the actual writeup that bugged me. Particularly the repeated references to the SCP as "the object" or "the entity."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't dislike it, but I don't particularly like it either. Can't really put my finger on why. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh hey, this thing. I like it when a very dangerous item is executed well.

To be specific: the containment procedures kick ass.
